findings.description: StreamingMedian.add uses insort which is O(n) per insertion, making n inserts O(n^2); a true streaming median (two heaps) would be O(log n) and the 'streaming' name oversells the implementation.
moving_average maintains a running sum incrementally, which can accumulate floating-point rounding error over long inputs versus recomputing each window sum.
running_total seeds the accumulator with 0.0, coercing integer inputs to float outputs, which is a subtle type-contract change for int-only data.
safe_mean sums floats without compensation (e.g. math.fsum), so large or ill-conditioned inputs lose precision.
clamp does not handle NaN value; max/min propagation with NaN yields order-dependent, undefined-feeling results rather than an explicit error.
percentile calls sorted() on the full input (O(n log n)); for single-percentile queries a selection/quickselect could be O(n), though this is a minor concern for dashboard sizes.
percentile bounds check accepts a NaN pct path inconsistently and relies on chained-comparison semantics; behavior for non-finite pct is implicit rather than documented.
moving_average accepts list[float] while sibling functions accept Iterable[float], an inconsistent parameter-typing style across the module.
